Don’t get scammed by fake job agents in Thailand.

If someone is promising you a “guaranteed job” and asking for heavy upfront fees, that’s your red flag.

If you are serious about working in Thailand, you can explore opportunities through established recruitment agencies that operate legally in Thailand:

• PRTR – https://www.prtr.com
• Adecco Thailand – https://www.adecco.co.th
• Manpower Thailand – https://www.manpowerthailand.com
• Michael Page Thailand – https://www.michaelpage.co.th
• JAC Recruitment Thailand – https://www.jac-recruitment.co.th

These are structured recruitment firms working with companies across corporate, hospitality, administration, IT and other industries.

Important:
Most international agencies do NOT charge candidates. They are paid by employers. Always verify job offers and check your visa eligibility before making any decision.

Save this post and follow for real guidance about working and living in Thailand as an Indian. #IndiansInThailand

Spicy tuna crispy rice…but stuffed 🤭

I’ve always loved crispy tuna rice and have made it so many times (I even shared an air fryer version before). But this idea randomly came to me when I was thinking about those deep fried stuffed Japanese sandos, and I had to try it… it just made sense to turn it into something you can actually stuff and hold like this.

It’s one of those ideas that just popped into my head and I couldn’t not make it.

And honestly… I like this version even more. The rice gets extra crispy since there’s more surface area, but the inside stays soft and a little chewy, so stuffing in cold spicy tuna just works so well. And you can fit a really good amount of spicy tuna inside (use a fork!), which makes every bite even more satisfying.

Plus everything stays in place, so it’s way easier (and more enjoyable) to eat without it falling apart like the usual version.

Same flavors, just a different format… but it works so well 🤭 save this if you love crispy tuna and let me know if you’d try this version 😍🙌

🍣Stuffed Spicy Tuna Crispy Rice

Makes ~8 pieces

- Sushi Rice
~3 cups cooked rice
4 Tbsp rice vinegar
1½ Tbsp sugar
½ Tbsp salt

- Spicy Tuna
¾ lb sushi-grade tuna, finely chopped
2.5 Tbsp Kewpie mayo
2 Tbsp sriracha
1 Tbsp soy sauce
½ Tbsp sesame oil
2 stalks green onion, chopped

- For Topping
Avocado, sliced
Jalapeño, sliced
Eel sauce (sweet soy glaze)
Sesame seeds

Instructions

1. Shape
Line a square pan with parchment (i used a 8”*8” brownie pan)
Spread rice evenly (about ¾” thick — thicker rice makes it easier to cut and stuff without breaking)
→ Chill for a few hours until firm

2. Cut
Slice into 4 squares, then cut diagonally to make 8 triangles

3. Fry
Deep fry until golden and crispy
Let cool slightly

4. Stuff
Cut a deep slit along the long edge
Gently open to create a pocket
Stuff with spicy tuna

5. Finish
Top with avocado and jalapeño
Drizzle eel sauce and sprinkle sesame seeds

#stuffedspicytunacrispyrice #stuffedcrispytuna

The goal is not to force yourself into fake optimism. It is to stop treating one negative outcome as certainty. When the mind narrows everything down to a single worst-case scenario, the body reacts as if that future is already real. But the moment you widen the frame and remember there are multiple possible outcomes, something shifts. You regain steadiness. You regain perspective. 

That is where discipline begins. 

Not in pretending everything will work out perfectly, but in refusing to let fear write the full story before reality has spoken.

What do today’s top marketing leaders really think about influencer marketing? #GoodAdsMatter

At the Good Ads Matter Brand CMOs’ and Marketing Heads’ Roundtable, Sunder Balasubramanian @sunderb78, Chief Marketing Officer at Myntra @myntra & Rajshree Bakshi @rajshree.bakshi, Senior Vice President of Marketing at Reliance Industries Limited @relianceupdates, shared their perspectives on the effectiveness of influencer marketing.

Sunder Balasubramanian recalled an incident that made him realise how sharply Gen Z picks up on content pushed through social media and influencers. He added that influencer marketing has played a huge role in shaping Myntra’s brand, particularly in building trust with younger consumers.

Rajshree Bakshi admitted that she was initially sceptical of influencer marketing. However, her perspective shifted after a conversation with a young individual who told her, “You know, we don’t follow products, we follow people.” This insight underscores the growing importance of community-driven influence and what Gen Z prioritises when it comes to credibility.

Panel Moderator: Abhijit Avasthi @abhijitavasthi | Founder of Sideways @sidewaysconsulting

George Gurdjieff (1866?–1949), 20. yüzyılın başında ortaya çıkan en tuhaf figürlerden biridir. Ermeni-Yunan kökenli bir öğretmen olarak, insanın “uyanık olduğunu zanneden uyuyan bir varlık” olduğunu iddia etti. Ona göre birey mekanikti; düşünceler, duygular ve hareketler otomatikti. “Dördüncü Yol” adını verdiği yaklaşımda manastır, fakir ya da yogi disiplinlerinin yerine gündelik yaşam içinde bilinç geliştirme pratiğini önerdi. Paris ve Fontainebleau’daki enstitüsünde sanat, müzik ve bedensel disiplinleri içeren karma bir öğretim sistemi kurdu. Metinleri özellikle Beelzebub’s Tales to His Grandson alegorik ve bilinçli olarak zorlayıcı bir dil kullanır; anlaşılmak için değil, zihinsel konforu bozmak için yazılmıştır.

Gurdjieff’in en dikkat çekici pratiği “Movements” olarak bilinen danslardır. Bu hareketler klasik dans değil; ritmik, simetrik ve çoğu zaman matematiksel olarak yapılandırılmış koreografilerdi. Amaç estetik değil, dikkat bölünmesini zorlamaktı: beden bir ritimde ilerlerken eller başka bir deseni izler, göz ve nefes ayrı bir disipline bağlanırdı. Bu eşzamanlılık, mekanikliği kırmak için tasarlanmıştı. Müziği çoğunlukla öğrencisi Thomas de Hartmann ile birlikte bestelenmişti ve Orta Asya ile Ortodoks kilise ezgilerinden izler taşır. Sahne performansından çok içsel bir laboratuvar niteliğindedir; dışarıdan bakıldığında asketik ve soğuk görünür, içeride ise dikkat mimarisi kurmaya yöneliktir.

QLIMNOX notu:
Gurdjieff’in dansı mistik değil; mekanik bilince karşı tasarlanmış bir koreografik müdahaledir. Estetik bir gösteri değil, bilinç mühendisliğidir.

I used to think being a «multi-tool creative» made me more versatile. And it does, but it also turned me into a full-time file manager.

You know the routine: Generate in one app. Download. Upload to another. Adjust. Export again. Move it somewhere else. By the time you’re done, you’ve forgotten what you were actually trying to create.

We’ve normalized this chaos. We call it «being resourceful.» But really? It’s just friction disguised as flexibility.

The best ideas don’t die from lack of talent. They die from exhaustion. From the mental overhead of stitching together five different platforms just to see one vision through.

What if the future of creativity isn’t about finding THE perfect tool, but about building ecosystems where tools actually work together? Where testing an idea doesn’t require opening 12 tabs and three different subscriptions?

That’s the shift. Not faster tools. Smarter systems.

Adobe Firefly Boards gets this. It’s not trying to replace your favorite AI models, it’s letting them finally talk to each other in one space. Test different tools side by side. Iterate without the export hell. Keep your momentum instead of killing it.

Your creativity deserves infrastructure that keeps up with your brain, not the other way around.

Philosopher Slavoj Žižek provocatively dismisses the concept of wisdom, calling it the "most conformist thing you can imagine." He contends that its true danger lies in its intellectual flexibility, arguing that a so-called "wise man" can validate any action or perspective, thus stripping the term of any meaningful critical or directional value. To demonstrate this, Žižek outlines four contradictory philosophical positions—from seeking eternity over fleeting pleasures, to embracing the present moment with Carpe Diem, to finding a synthesis between the two, and finally to simply accepting the inherent contradiction—showing that each can be presented and sold as legitimate "wisdom." His ultimate point is that this label is often just a tool for justification, rendering it intellectually suspect and a refuge for those avoiding genuine engagement with difficult choices.

Meet Shamnad Basheer, the mastermind behind Increasing Diversity by Increasing Access.

In 2010, he founded IDIA with a simple, unwavering belief that the law belongs to everyone. 

This was his way of opening doors to reaching the remote corners of India. He found potential where few were looking and began creating pathways where none existed.

This is our attempt to let you hear him. The voice that still carries the spirit of every life changed, every barrier broken and every student who now walks into a courtroom knowing they belong.

This is the mind that built IDIA and he remains at the heart of it.

Zero oil creamy protein rich gravy💚 Zero oil gravies episode 17 
Zero oil. Maximum creaminess. 💚✨
Who said comfort food can’t be healthy? This protein-rich green moong gravy is silky, satisfying, and completely guilt-free and protein rich 🤌

Clean eating never tasted this good!
✨ Serve with roti, dosa, parotta, or rice — wholesome, filling & delicious!

1.Soak 1 cup green moong overnight. Pressure cook with 1 cup water until soft. Keep aside.
2. In the same cooker, add 250 g yellow pumpkin, 1 carrot, 1 onion, and 1 almond. Cook until soft.
3. Transfer to a mixer. Add 200 g low-fat paneer + ½ cup makhana and grind to a smooth paste.
4. Pour this paste into a pan. Add 1 tsp salt, 1 tsp chilli powder, 1 tsp garam masala, and required water.
5. Cook until the raw smell goes and it turns creamy.
6. Add the cooked green moong and mix well.
7. Finish with fresh coriander leaves 🌿

Happy Eating 💕

🌴🔥 HORCHATA DE ARROZ MEXICANA 

🧾 Ingredientes:
🍚 1 taza de arroz blanco
💧 2 tazas de agua (para remojar)
🥛 2 tazas de leche (entera o vegetal)
🍶 1/2 taza de leche condensada
🌿 1 raja de canela
💦 1 cucharadita de vainilla (opcional)

👩‍🍳 Paso a paso:
1️⃣ Lava el arroz 🍚 y déjalo en remojo con el agua 💧 y la canela 🌿 durante mínimo 4 horas (mejor toda la noche).

2️⃣ Licúa el arroz con el agua del remojo y la canela hasta que quede bien triturado.

3️⃣ Cuela la mezcla con un colador fino o tela, exprimiendo bien para sacar todo el sabor.

4️⃣ Añade la leche 🥛, la leche condensada 🍶 y la vainilla 💦. Mezcla hasta que quede homogéneo.

5️⃣ Refrigera mínimo 2 horas ❄️ (clave porque no lleva hielo, así queda bien fría y más espesa).

6️⃣ Sirve bien fría 🥶 y, si quieres, espolvorea un toque de canela por encima.

Easy palappam 

❌❌No Repost❌❌

- Roasted Rice Flour/ Normal Rice flour 1 cup
- Grated coconut 1/2 Cup
- Soaked Rice flakes or cooked rice 1/4 cup
- yeast 1/4 Tsp
- Salt
- sugar 1 Tbsp
- Warm water 3/4 to 1 cup ( as required)

Grind together roasted appam flour, grated coconut, soaked poha, yeast, salt, sugar, and warm water
(Warm water helps speed up fermentation)
	•	Blend into a smooth batter
	•	Allow it to ferment for 1–2 hours
(Mine took around 1.5 hours to rise nicely)
	•	Heat an appam pan, pour a ladle of batter, and gently swirl the pan to spread it
	•	Once bubbles start forming, cover with a lid and let it cook
	•	Remove once done and serve hot with your favorite curry

✨ Tip: You can place the batter over a bowl of hot water to speed up fermentation

A fifteen-year web performance problem got solved this week by one developer and a few kilobytes of code.

Every time a browser measures text height, it triggers a reflow, a full recalculation of the page layout. Do that for a hundred elements at once and your UI stutters. Developers have known about this forever and mostly just worked around it.

Pretext skips the DOM entirely. It uses Canvas as a measurement reference, runs the expensive part once on prepare, and after that layout is pure arithmetic. Five hundred text measurements in nineteen milliseconds. Each layout call after that in under a tenth of a millisecond, roughly five hundred times faster than traditional DOM approaches.

The hard part was accuracy across browsers. Korean, Arabic, emojis, whitespace edge cases, platform quirks in Safari vs Chrome vs Firefox. Previous Canvas-only approaches always drifted from real browser output on these. Cheng Lou spent weeks running Claude against actual browser rendering until the results matched.

Current limitations worth knowing: does not cover every CSS text property, system-ui font can be inaccurate on macOS, and text rendered to Canvas needs an overlay to be selectable. All documented, all narrow.

The thread hit 7.7 million views in twenty-four hours. Vercel CEO and shadcn both replied. People are already shipping masonry grids, real-time text wrapping, and chat bubble layouts that size correctly on first render.
#ai #artificialintelligence #technews #chatgpt #claude

Peer-to-peer cricket action for the lads wasn’t on my bingo card…

Every six months, a new cricket league appears out of nowhere.

UAE. Caribbean. South Africa. The U.S.

And everyone asks the wrong question:
“Why are billionaires funding cricket where nobody watches cricket?”

Here’s the answer.

They’re not building sports leagues.
They’re building gambling laboratories.

Since 2018, U.S. sports betting has legalized state by state.
Every new state is a new market.
But you can’t experiment aggressively in regulated environments.

So the testing happens elsewhere.

T20 cricket is perfect for this.

A match lasts three hours.
Every ball is a betting event.

Two innings.
120 balls each.
That’s 240 live betting moments per game — more than the NFL.

Micro-bets.
Same-game parlays.
Live cash-outs.

Perfect for training algorithms.

So the play is simple.

Launch leagues in loosely regulated markets.
Integrate betting from day one.
Collect massive behavioral data.

Then deploy those systems in the U.S.

This already happened.

Big Bash League normalized parlays and cash-outs.
New York legalized betting in 2022.
DraftKings and FanDuel launched with the exact same features.

Same tech.
Same playbook.

The Indian Premier League is the biggest lab of all.
Ten-billion-dollar league.
Billions wagered offshore every season.

And now Major League Cricket in the U.S.
Owned by tech CEOs.
Marketed as “growing the sport.”

But the real product isn’t cricket.

It’s betting data.

Cricket leagues aren’t about fans.
They’re about training the system.

Cricket was never the business.

The data was.

#sports #cricket #businessideas #business #wealth

Every day I verify KYC for Pay Later & Credit Cards.

Hundreds of people…
Signing up for more credit.

But what shocks me the most is this:

Most of them already know
AI is disrupting jobs.

Still… people are stacking more debt on themselves.

Maybe it’s convenience.
Maybe it’s pressure.
Or maybe we’re just ignoring the risk.

No judgement here.

Just a question —

Are we preparing for the future…
or borrowing from it?

Just give it a thought.

✨ Hanging upside down has so many benefits. (Not to be done with certain ailments,  high blood pressure, vertigo, and headaches and always best to do with an experienced teacher) And though it’s not exactly like practicing headstand, you can still get a lot out of it. 

✨ It can improve circulation and heart heath! Inverting can lower the heart’s workload and can lower your resting heart rate 

✨It can decompress the spine! It creates space and room between the vertebrae which can help relieve back pain, improve posture, and rehydrate the discs

✨It can promote lymphatic drainage. The lymphatic system required gravity and movement to drain!

✨Inverting promotes healthy digestion! It can super helpful if you are constipated or experiencing bloating. Turning everything upside down really gets things moving! 

✨ It can improve focus and clarity. Reversing blood flow increases the amount of oxygen that goes to the brain. 

✨ It can be a mood booster! Going upside down activates the parasympathetic nervous system which lower the hormones of stress and increases the “feel-good” neurotransmitters!! 

✨ And you can get all of these benefits from hanging upside down 🙃 

Want to experience the ropes?

Kunafa in Airfryer. Visit My YouTube Channel ( Hot&Sweet Rasoi) 
For Detailed Recipe..
Kunafa Recipe
Ingredients:

🔹Kunafa dough (half quantity, shredded)
🔹¼ cup melted butter
🔹¼ cup ghee
🔹1 cup milk
🔹1 tbsp cornflour
🔹1 tbsp milk powder
🔹1 tsp sugar
🔹2 tbsp cream cheese
🔹1 tsp salted butter
🔹1 cup mozzarella cheese
🔹1 cup sugar
🔹1 cup water
🔹1 slice lemon

Process:

🔹Shred kunafa dough, mix with melted butter & ghee. Keep aside.

🔹In a pan, mix milk, cornflour, milk powder & sugar. Cook till thick.

🔹Add cream cheese & salted butter, mix well and switch off flame.

🔹For syrup, cook sugar + water till slightly sticky. Add lemon slice, keep aside.

🔹Grease a 7-inch cake tin with 1 tbsp ghee + 1 tbsp butter.

🔹Add half kunafa dough, press well.

🔹Add prepared cream, then mozzarella cheese.

🔹Cover with remaining kunafa dough.

🔹Place tin on a heavy pan, cook on low flame for 10 mins.

🔹Flip and cook other side for 10 mins.

🔹Pour cooled sugar syrup on top.

✔️ Kunafa is ready! ✨

SEO is obsolete in 2026, and this tool is solving ranking in the AI era. 

Called Crowd reply, they track how ChatGPT, Claude, and Gemini rank your brand and whether they mention your name in queries or not. Then they find pages like Reddit threads which these AI models cite as sources. 

And then, here's where it gets dark, they inject your brand name into these threads through a managed network of persona-based Reddit accounts. Accounts with good karma designed to look like real people. And the replies made by these accounts are pushed to the top through coordinated upvotes. 

And just after that, these chatbots will organically start ranking your company's name among the top brands. Please share this around.

HOW TO MAKE PERFECT COUSCOUS 👇🏽
Step 1: Don’t trust the back of your couscous box.
Step 2: Follow this recipe 👇🏽

2 cups (340g) dry couscous
1 ½ cups (360g) room-temp broth or water
2 tbsp olive oil or melted butter
Salt, to taste

1️⃣ Preheat oven to 350℉ (175℃). Transfer couscous to an oven-safe dish or pan. 
2️⃣ Pour broth/water over and stir. Mix to combine and let sit 10–15 min until liquid is absorbed.
3️⃣ Fluff with fork or fingers until light and separated.
4️⃣ Add olive oil (or butter) + salt. Mix well.
5️⃣ Cover and bake for 30 min, until hot and fully tender.

✨ Serve immediately.

Shed at @indiaartfair – Tambour Console

The Tambour Console grew from Shed’s study of scaffolding as a structural system. Temporary scaffolding seen across construction sites uses bamboo as repetitive members tied together to form frameworks that can expand, shift and adapt easily. This logic was translated into a furniture scale as a modular framework.

Within this structure sits a tambour shutter. While traditionally made from flat slats, we reinterpreted the system using cylindrical wooden members linked by rope. To enable its movement, a recessed guide track was engineered into the timber frame built specifically to navigate the round geometry of the members.  This allows the woven surface to glide through the corners of the framework in one fluid, tactile motion. The console avoids adhesive construction and was assembled using brass joinery and mechanical connections, allowing it to be taken apart, repaired or adapted over time. 

The Tambour Console is part of a larger study of vernacular construction systems, a logic of assembly also explored in the Octa Table and Mattress Sofa.

Recipe ⤵️ One of the MOST addicting sauces that I put in almost all my food! Salsa Macha 😩🌶️

Ingredients:
- 2-3 guajillo 
- 2 ancho
- 1/2 c chile de árbol (about a large handful)
- 2 tbsp sesame seeds 
- 1 tbsp pumpkin seeds (peeled)
- 2 c neutral oil 
- 1 tsp onion powder
- 1/2 tsp msg
- 2 tsp apple cider vinegar 
- 1 corn tortilla 

Directions:
1. In a large skillet heat about 2c oil over medium heat.
2. Fry your garlic & tortillas in batches until golden brown. 
3. Then fry your peppers. Followed by both seeds (separately). All of these are gonna fry for 15-20 seconds they’re gonna fry super quick!
4. Allow oil to cool a bit. Add everything to a blender with about 1 c of, onion powder, msg, 2 tsp salt, and pulse until you get a nice chunky texture. If it’s having a hard time blending slowly add in more oil. 
5. Add to desired container, finish with vinegar, and adjust salt to taste. 
6. Serve with literally anything and enjoy babe !

Not just dessert… it’s a whole mood 🌙

🥥 Ingredients
3 tbsp gasagase (poppy seeds)
1 tbsp rice
3 tbsp dry coconut (or fresh coconut)
6–8 cashews
6–8 almonds
1/2 or 3/4 cup jaggery
1/2–ltr milk
2–3 cardamom pods
1 tsp ghee
Cashews & raisins for garnish

Dry roast gasagase until aromatic, then lightly roast dry coconut and rice separately until they release a nutty fragrance. Let everything cool, then add the roasted ingredients to a mixer along with cashews, almonds, cardamom, and milk, and grind into a smooth, creamy paste. Transfer this paste to a pan, add milk, and cook on medium flame, stirring continuously as it thickens. Once cooked, add jaggery and mix well, allowing it to simmer for a few minutes. Adjust the consistency with more milk or water if needed, and finish by topping with ghee-roasted cashews and raisins.

Zero oil chana white kuruma❣️ Zero oil gravies episode 15 ❣️ 
This zero oil white chana kurma is undoubtedly one of the easiest and tastiest dish you can make. I have made 14 no oil recipes and some people asked me to make it even without coconut, so here you go. It is curated such that no coconut is needed but still tastes exactly like kurma. Save and share this reel to your family!
1. In a pressure cook add 1 cup soaked chana (overnight) , 1 onion, 1 tomato , 1 tsp salt and cook for 5 whistles 
2. Keep some cooked chana aside 1/4 cup and add the balance to a pan.
3. For kurma, grind together 3 garlic , 1 inch ginger, 1.5 tbsp roasted gram , 1 tsp fennel seeds , 1.5 tsp poppy seeds, 1/4 cup boiled chana , cinnamon, cloves 2 and 1 cardamom. Grind well and add it to the pan
4. Add handful of mint and coriander and serve hot with pulka , roti , poori , idiyappam etc. Enjoy!!

Mouri Potol ❤️ Recipe ⬇️ 
📌 Start by roasting and coarsely pounding Mouri/saunf/fennel seeds. Don’t roast beyond a minute on low flame. 
📌 In a Kadhai shallow fry chopped parwal and aloo separately 
📌 In the same Kadhai add mustard oil
📌 Add a tempering of dry red chilli, bay leaf, cardomom, cinnamon, cloves 
📌 Add ginger chilli paste, followed by a tbsp of pounded saunf, haldi and chilli powder
📌 Add a bit of water and mix well 
📌 When the spices release oil add the shallow fried parwal and potatoes and fry well 
📌 Add a small bowl of milk (approx 8-10 tbsp), some water and let it all come to a boil 
📌 Prepare a tadka if ghee and tbsp of pounded saunf and pour it over the curry and serve hot ❤️

Sold your ESOPs? You might not have to pay any tax on it

Comment “ESOP” if you want a step-by-step breakdown 🔍

Here’s the catch most people miss 👇

When you sell ESOPs, the gains are taxed.
But under Section 54F, if you reinvest those gains into a residential property within the timeline, your tax liability can go to zero.

• Sell ESOPs → generate long-term capital gains
• Reinvest in a residential house → claim exemption
• No restriction to just first-time buyers
• Benefit available up to ₹10 crore

The real game? Timing.
Hold your ESOPs long enough, exit strategically, and reinvest within the window.

Supple wide rice noodle rolls, street food style!

Comment LUNAR for the recipe sent straight to your DM's.

You’ll need:
• 50 g Rice flour
• 30 g Tapioca starch
• 1 g Kosher salt
• 250 g Water

Cheung fun times:

  1. Whisk together rice flour, tapioca starch, and salt until well combined. Add water and whisk until smooth. Strain batter through fine-mesh strainer into second large bowl, rest 10 mins - 2 days. If resting more than 30 mins, put in fridge.

2. In 12-inch diameter pot, bring 3-4 inches of water to a simmer over medium-high heat. Set 12-inch bamboo steamer on top. Spray sheet pan with non stick in even layer. Set in steamer, cover, and preheat 1 min.

3. Uncover steamer, ladle 55g batter into prepared sheet pan, tilt for even layer. Cover and cook until batter sets and turns opaque and very bubbly at the surface.

4. Carefully transfer sheet pan to a heatproof surface. Let stand for 1 min. Use scraper, push cooked batter to one edge of sheet pan. Cut crosswise, transfer to plate, cover and set aside. Repeat steps 2-4.

5. Return all rolls to steamer, cover and reheat 2 mins. Garnish plate of rice rolls with toppings and condiments, as desired. Serve immediately.

Soy sauce, garlic, shallots, top with anything you like!

lebeniye çorbası • ramadan recipes ep 6

{a creamy yogurt soup with rice, chickpeas and tiny meatballs 🥣 comforting but still light}

recipe:

ingredients:

meatballs -

300 gm ground beef (you can also use ground chicken or lamb if you prefer)
salt to taste
1 tsp black pepper
1 tsp red pepper flakes (i used hot aleppo pepper, skip this altogether if you don’t like spice)
1 small onion grated 🧅
1/2 tsp dried parsley

for the soup:

2 tbsp rice
1 cup cooked chickpeas
4 cups water

for the yogurt mixture -

1 1/2 cups plain yogurt (make sure it’s not sour)
1 egg yolk
2 tbsp flour
juice of 1/2 lemon 🍋
1 tbsp grated garlic 

for finishing -

2-3 tbsp butter 🧈
1 tbsp dried mint

method:

1. in a bowl, mix ground beef, grated onion, salt, dried parsley, red chili flakes and black pepper. knead it well for a few minutes so the mixture comes together.
2. make very small meatballs, about marble sized. set aside.
3. in a pot, add rice and water and cook until the rice softens.
4. meanwhile melt butter in a pan and add the meatballs. cook until lightly browned. add the chickpeas, dried mint, salt to taste. mix and set aside.
5. in a bowl whisk together yogurt, egg yolk, flour, lemon juice and garlic until smooth.
6. take a couple ladles of the hot broth from the rice pot and slowly mix it into the yogurt mixture to temper it.
7. pour the yogurt mixture into the pot while stirring continuously and bring it to a gentle boil.
8. add the browned meatballs into the soup and mix and enjoy warm!

Kimamk Sewaiyan (Ammi s Recipe) ✨

Some recipes are more than just food—they’re memories. This Qimami Sewaiyan recipe is having a lot of nostalgia (do remember my Ammi in your prayers she’s no more 💔)
Before marriage, I had never made it myself, but every year after marriage I call my mom, follow her instructions, and it turns out absolutely perfect every single time. A truly guaranteed recipe that never fails!

Ingredients:
	•	½ kg fine Banarasi Sewaiyan
	•	1 kg sugar
	•	150-200 g ghee
	•	100 g Cashews
	•	50 g Almonds
	•	1/2 Dry coconut (gola)
	•	6-7 green Cardamom
	•	50-60 gm Makhana
	•	250 gm khoya
 • 1 litre milk or water for chashni
 -1 tsp kewra water 

Method:
	1.	First take very fine Banarasi sewaiyan and lightly roast them. Then remove them in a bowl.
	2.	In a pan, heat ghee and add cardamom 
	3.	Add sugar and prepare the syrup using milk or water.
	4.	Cook the syrup on low flame for about 2 hours until it becomes thick and reaches the qeemam-like consistency.
	5.	Now add the roasted sewaiyan, roasted khoya, and all the dry fruits.
	6.	Mix everything well and turn off the flame.
	7.	Let it set for about 1 hour — and your perfectly delicious Qeemami Sewaiyan are ready to serve! ✨

A recipe straight from My kitchen to my Eid table. 🤍

✨ Omm Ali أم علي ✨

Without a doubt… the best Egyptian dessert.
Ali’s mom.

🥐 Directions

1️⃣ Heat the milk
Add 4 cups milk, 3/4 cup sugar, and 1 teaspoon vanilla extract to a pot and heat until the sugar dissolves.
‎حطي ٤ أكواب لبن مع ¾ كوب سكر ومعلقة صغيرة فانيليا على النار لحد ما السكر يذوب.

2️⃣ Prepare the croissants
Use 8–10 croissants and cut them into pieces slightly larger than bite size. They’ll shrink once they soak up the milk in the oven.
‎قطعي ٨–١٠ كرواسون لقطع أكبر شوية من حجم اللقمة لأنهم هيصغروا في الفرن.

3️⃣ Prepare the cream
If you don’t have eshta, whip 1 cup heavy cream until it thickens. This will go on top later.
‎لو معندكش قشطة اخفقي كوب كريمة لحد ما تتقل شوية.

4️⃣ Prepare the makasarat
Use slivered almonds, pistachios, chopped walnuts, and golden raisins.
‎جهزي المكسرات: لوز شرائح، فستق، جوز مفروم، وزبيب دهبي.

5️⃣ Assemble
In a deep tray, layer croissants, makasarat, then croissants again until everything is used. Finish with makasarat on top.
‎في صينية عميقة رصي كرواسون ثم مكسرات ثم كرواسون مرة تانية لحد ما تخلصي وخلصي بمكسرات على الوش.

6️⃣ Add the milk
Pour the hot milk mixture over everything and press down gently so the croissants absorb it. If the milk doesn’t reach the top, add a little more regular milk.
‎صبي خليط اللبن السخن فوق الكرواسون واضغطي عليه بهدوء عشان يشرب. لو اللبن موصلش للوش زودي شوية لبن عادي.

7️⃣ Add the cream
Spread the eshta or whipped heavy cream over the top.

There are some dishes that carry history in every spoon. Unde Kaima Saar is one of them.

A dish whispered about in the kitchens of Queen Soyarabai, a Maratha queen whose roots trace back to Karnataka. In those royal kitchens, food was not just nourishment—it was strength for a Maratha warrior, fuel for long rides, battles, and the relentless rhythm of empire.

Kaima means minced meat. Kheema as many know it today. But this preparation is different. The kheema is shaped into soft unde—little dumplings of kaima—that slowly simmer in a deeply spiced saar. The broth becomes rich, fiery, and comforting all at once.

It is the kind of Indian food that feels ancient. Simple ingredients, bold flavour, and a technique passed through memory rather than cookbooks.

This kaima saar sits at the crossroads of cultures—Maratha history and coastal Karnataka kitchens. A dish that reminds us how food travels with people, warriors, queens, and migrations.

Today we call these easy recipes or quick recipes, but centuries ago this was practical warrior food. Kheema cooks quickly. Saar stretches the dish. Kaima dumplings make it hearty. Perfect for feeding hungry soldiers and royal households alike.

Recipes like unde kaima saar are part of our heritage. These are the lost recipes that quietly survived in family kitchens long after royal courts disappeared.

If you love Indian food, dishes like this remind us that every plate holds a story. Of a Maratha warrior, a queen, and the powerful simplicity of kheema, kaima, and a deeply comforting saar.

Sometimes the most beautiful heritage lies hidden in our lost recipes.

And sometimes, the best quick recipes are actually centuries old.

Pet Architecture 
In this home, every design decision considered the three little residents who move through it every day.
Homes are often designed around people. This one was also designed around its three cats.
A dedicated climber connects the living space to a cat den positioned at lintel level, allowing the pets to move freely, perch above the room, and observe the house from their own lookout. The den is designed with an openable feature, making cleaning and maintenance simple while keeping the element seamlessly integrated into the architecture.
Rather than treating pet-friendly features as an afterthought, they became part of the spatial planning itself, influencing the placement of furniture, material choices, and the overall movement within the home.
A small design decision, but one that makes the home truly shared.

Would your pets enjoy a space like this? 🐾

This is NOT chia seed pudding…

This is aleev (halim) kheer — India’s own iron-rich superfood dessert 🤍

Aleev seeds are naturally rich in iron and folate, traditionally consumed to support hormonal balance and strength, especially during postpartum recovery. In many Indian homes, this kheer is given to new mothers as a nourishing, rebuilding food.

Recipe (1–2 servings):
• 30g aleev seeds
• 130g water (for soaking)
• 4 tbsp jaggery (adjust to taste)
• 1 tsp cardamom + nutmeg powder
• 2–2.5 cups milk
Please note that these measurements are for a minimum of 8 servings. Consume in moderation. 

Soak aleev seeds in water for 30 minutes until they swell.
In a pan, add jaggery and the soaked aleev seeds and roast gently.
Add cardamom and nutmeg powder.
Turn off the heat and let the mixture cool slightly (important before adding milk).
Add milk, mix well, and serve chilled.

Rooted in tradition, simple ingredients, deeply nourishing 🤍

Save this for when you want dessert with purpose.

Why's Nobody Talking About These Indians Films?

There’s a whole side of Indian cinema that exists quietly, away from the noise, away from the marketing, away from the familiar faces that dominate screens every Friday, films that weren’t built to please everyone at once but to observe, to disturb, to sit with people who are rarely centered in stories, films that feel like they were made because someone had something urgent to say rather than something profitable to sell. What connects these is not language or region or even style, it’s the stubborn refusal to simplify life into neat arcs, the willingness to stay in uncomfortable spaces where characters don’t become heroes and endings don’t hand you relief. You move from decaying cities to forgotten villages, from lonely interiors to chaotic streets, from fragile minds to violent impulses, and somewhere along the way you start realizing how vast this cinema really is, how many textures of reality it has captured without asking for permission to exist.

Some of these feel raw and almost confrontational, like they were shot with the fear that no one might fund something this honest again, others feel strangely tender, observing small humiliations, private dreams, and quiet desperation with a patience mainstream storytelling rarely allows.

1. Astaxanthin (the “internal sunscreen” carotenoid) - Human RCTs + meta-analyses suggest modest improvements in oxidative stress / some inflammation markers, and skin metrics in trials. 

2. Pterostilbene (resveratrol’s tougher cousin) - More lipophilic, longer half-life, and reported higher oral bioavailability vs resveratrol in human data. 

3. Magnesium L-Threonate (brain magnesium) - MIT-linked work put it on the map (brain Mg + synapses), and newer human trials report improvements on some cognition measures over weeks. 

4. Spermidine (autophagy signal) - The theory is strong. The human data is mixed — including a large RCT that didn’t move primary cognition outcomes. 

5. Photobiomodulation via Red and Near-Infrared light (the non-pill “supplement”) - ‘PBM’ is studied as a mitochondrial signal (cytochrome-c-oxidase / redox signaling → ATP), with human trial evidence in skin/recovery contexts using specific wavelengths. 
This is why I built AuroraBox: consistent output, consistent wavelengths, consistent routine.

If you want the exact stack order + routine, comment START and I’ll send it.

🌿🛒 Bhang Online in India? Here’s What the Law Says

With Holi around the corner, several brands like The #Bhang Store, Aqua Herbals, Bhang Baba, and Golden Patel Son are listing bhang-based products online — from bhang goli and powder to infused sweets.

But what does Indian law say? 👇

Under the Narcotic Drugs and Psychotropic Substances (NDPS) Act, 1985, cannabis resin (charas) and flowering tops (ganja) are illegal. However, cannabis leaves and seeds are not included in the prohibited definition, which is why traditional bhang (made from leaves) is treated differently.

⚖️ Important to remember:
• States can regulate or restrict bhang sales
• Only licensed vendors are permitted in certain states
• Online sale may fall under state excise laws
• Driving or public nuisance under influence is punishable

Cultural tradition meets legal boundaries — so always verify your state’s excise rules before purchasing or consuming.

Celebrate responsibly this Holi. 🌈

Boot Ka Bhabra ❤️ Recipe ⬇️
Crispy on the outside, soft and hearty inside, Boot ka Bhabra is a rustic Holi special made with fresh hara chana. Simple ingredients, bold texture and pure seasonal flavour.
Wishing you all a very Happy Holi filled with colour, chaos and really good food. 🌸
🥣 Ingredients
Besan – 1 cup
Rice flour – 1 cup
Fresh hara chana also called boot – 1 cup, whole
Onion – 1 medium, finely chopped
Green chilli – 1 to 2, finely chopped
Ginger garlic paste – 1 teaspoon
Fresh dhaniya – 2 tablespoons, finely chopped
Ajwain – ½ teaspoon
Black pepper powder – ½ teaspoon
Haldi – ½ teaspoon
Red chilli powder – 1 teaspoon
Salt – as per taste
Water – as needed
Mustard oil – for cooking
👩🏻‍🍳 Method
📌 In a large bowl, mix besan and rice flour.
📌 Add ajwain, chopped onion, green chilli, ginger garlic paste, fresh dhaniya, black pepper powder, haldi, red chilli powder and salt.
📌 Add the whole fresh hara chana and mix everything well.
📌 Gradually add water in batches and whisk to form a thick but spreadable batter. It should not be runny.
📌 Heat mustard oil in a flat pan or tawa. Once hot, lower the flame.
📌 Pour a ladleful of batter and gently spread into a thick pancake. Cook on slow flame till golden and crisp. Flip and cook the other side till evenly browned.
📌 Serve hot with green chutney or kasundi. 😍
Note: You can add dhaniya powder or other curry spices if you like, but I prefer keeping it simple so the flavour of fresh boot really shines.

A happy cat is one that gets to follow their instincts, here’s how to make that happen at home 👇

When we invite them into our homes, we’re giving them shelter, love, and care… but don’t forget 👉we also need to honor their natural behaviours such as hunting, foraging, problem-solving, and stalking. Without outlets for these instincts, even the sweetest cat can get bored, frustrated, or overstimulated.

That’s why enrichment is so important! Here are 5 simple DIY activities that let your cat exercise their natural instincts while keeping them mentally and physically stimulated:

1️⃣ Flip the Lid – Tap or flip the lid to release treats. Builds problem-solving and cause-and-effect understanding.
2️⃣ Jump & Drop Flap – Jump or reach to tap the flap. Strengthens coordination, vertical movement, and paw precision.
3️⃣ Paper Treasure Hunt – Hunt for treats hidden under paper pieces. Encourages scent work and slows down eating.
4️⃣ Egg Carton Hunt – Work compartment by compartment to get the treats. Teaches patience and focus.
5️⃣ Treat Tube Drop – Reach in or manipulate the roll to release treats. Excellent for persistence, movement, and problem-solving.

Every activity taps into your cat’s natural instincts, keeps their brain active, and prevents boredom-driven behaviours, all while being simple and fun to set up.

💡 Remember: Play isn’t just fun, it’s instinct fulfillment. A mentally satisfied cat is a happier, calmer cat.

✨ SAVE this post for your next enrichment session, SHARE it with a fellow cat parent, and follow @thebengalguide for more tips to keep your cat happy, healthy, and mentally stimulated!

Slow-cooked chicken in a deep red masala that tastes even better the next day. Save this for your next comfort meal ✨

Hyderabadi Wedding-Style Red Chicken Curry 
Makes 4 servings of 250 g each 
~ 347 Kcal | 20 G P | 25 g F | 12 g C per serving 
Ingredients (using ~500 g bone-in chicken)
Nut & coconut paste
	•	10–15 cashews
	•	10 almonds
	•	3 tbsp desiccated coconut
	•	Water, as needed
Marinade
	•	500 g bone-in chicken
	•	½ cup thick curd
	•	1½ tbsp ginger-garlic paste
	•	2–3 slit green chilies
	•	1 cup browned onions
 - Thinly slice 2 large onions, toss with a few drops of oil, microwave on high for 10-12 mins, stirring every two mins, until golden OR air fry at 180°C for 10–12 mins until browned.
	•	Handful mint leaves
	•	Handful coriander leaves
	•	1 tbsp green chili sauce
	•	1 tbsp red chili sauce
	•	2 tsp dark soy sauce
	•	¼ tsp turmeric powder
	•	2–3 tbsp Kashmiri chili powder (for color - adjust acc to spice)
	•	1 tbsp coriander powder
	•	1 tsp garam masala
	•	Salt & pepper to taste
	•	1 tbsp lemon juice
	•	2 tsp ghee

For cooking
	•	1 tbsp neutral oil
	•	Few curry leaves
	•	2 tsp fresh cream 
	•	Chopped coriander leaves

Method
1️⃣ Blend cashews, almonds, desiccated coconut, and water into a smooth paste.
2️⃣ In a large bowl, combine the nut paste with all the marinade ingredients.
3️⃣ Add chicken and coat well.
Marinate for at least 2 hours, or overnight for best flavor.
4️⃣ Heat oil in a heavy pan. Add curry leaves and let them crackle.
5️⃣ Transfer the entire marinated chicken into the pan. Cook on low–medium heat, covered, for 40–45 minutes, stirring occasionally so the masala doesn’t burn.
6️⃣ Once oil separates, the gravy deepens to a darker red, and the chicken becomes tender and almost fall-off-the-bone, add fresh cream and chopped coriander. Switch off heat.
7️⃣ Rest for 10 minutes before serving. Serve with soft roti or rice.
⭐ Tip
This curry tastes even better after resting 1–2 hours or when reheated later — the masala settles and deepens beautifully.

.
(Hyderabadi red chicken curry, shaadi wala chicken, wedding style chicken curry, Indian chicken gravy)
.

Macros (4 Tbsp Serving): 48 Calories | 4gP | 3.5gC | 2gF (Makes ~8 Servings)

Ingredients:
- 300g (1¼ cups) Nonfat Greek Yogurt
- 60g (¼ cup) Light Mayo
- 2 tsp Distilled White Vinegar
- 38g (¼ cup) Nacho Cheddar Powder (Kernel Season’s works great, can be found in most popcorn sections or online)
- 2 tsp Paprika
- 1.5 tsp Chili Powder
- 1 tsp Cayenne
- 1 tsp garlic powder
- 1 tsp onion powder
- ½ tsp fine salt
- ¼ tsp MSG (optional but recommended)
- 2 tsp Sugar free sweetener (1-1 substitute like Monkfruit)
- NOTE: Seasonings are suggestions and your exact flavor preference may vary. Season with your heart and adjust to your liking!

Instructions:
- Add all ingredients to a jar
- Mix until smooth
- Taste and adjust seasonings as needed for your desired flavor (seasonings are guidelines, everyone taste preferences are different and you should always taste and adjust!)
- Adjust thickness by adding 1-2 tablespoons of water at a time until you get your desired consistency
- For best results, let it sit in the fridge for 15–30 minutes for flavors to meld
- Serve as a dip or dressing!

A little miso life hack! ✨ How do you use this type of miso that comes in a bag? 

Many people simply cut the top and use it as it is. But the bag often gets sticky and difficult to handle later, right? 

Instead, try cutting it right in the middle. Insert the tip of a knife first — it makes cutting much easier. 

Place it in a container, cover the surface with plastic wrap to prevent it from drying out, and close the lid. This way, it’s much easier to scoop with a spoon! 

Give it a try and make using miso more simple and enjoyable in your daily cooking. 😊

P.S.- Today’s miso is Kyo Koji Miso (means "Kyoto Koji Miso") from Honda Miso Honten in Kyoto — it has a fruity aroma and a delicate, elegant flavor!�

spicy kimchi tofu rice paper rolls🌶️🍚 the components of our bowl version but wrapped in rice paper and heavilyyy dunked in our fav kimchi mayo (save for the recipe)

makes about 6 rolls

For the tofu:
1 block firm or extra-firm tofu, cut into rectangular strips
2 tbsps kimchi juice
1 tbsp sesame oil
1 tbsp maple syrup
1 tbsp tamari
1 tbsp dark soy sauce
1 tbsp rice vinegar
1 tbsp kimchi hot sauce or sriracha
1 tsp cornstarch (to be whisked into reserved marinade)

Bring a pot of generously salted water to a boil. Add the cut tofu and boil gently for 5 minutes. Drain and pat dry. In a bowl, whisk together the kimchi juice, sesame oil, maple syrup, tamari, dark soy, rice vinegar, and hot sauce. Add the tofu and let it marinate for 10 minutes.

Heat a thin layer of avocado oil in a pan on medium heat. Remove the tofu from the marinade (reserve the leftover marinade) and cook in a single layer for 3 to 4 minutes on one side until golden, then flip and cook another 2 minutes. Lower the heat. Whisk the cornstarch into the reserved marinade, then pour into the pan and cook for 1 minute until the sauce thickens and coats the tofu.

For the pickled veg and mayo:
Combine matchstick cut radishes and cucumber with rice vinegar, tamari, chili crisp, chopped cilantro, sprinkle of sugar and salt. Shake and leave in fridge until ready. Make the kimchi mayo by combining 1/4 cup vegan mayo, 2 tablespoons chopped kimchi, 1 tablespoon kimchi hot sauce or sriracha, and zest of 1 lime.

For the rolls:
cooked rice
roasted seaweed sheets
rice paper 

To assemble, dunk a sheet of rice paper in cool water for 5-7 seconds. Lay on parchment or a clean dish towel. In the middle, layer 2 pieces of seaweed, white rice, tofu strips (3-4), cucumber, and radish. Roll like a burrito, as tightly as possible, tucking the sides as you go. Repeat x6. Serve with kimchi mayo!!

Meet "dört kaşık çorbası" aka "four spoon soup". Because all you need is one spoon each of green lentils, red lentils, rice & wheat (or bulgur). Super simple, but madddd comforting while it's snowing outside. 

Well, it starts with four spoons, but we end it with a silkyy, lemony yogurt 'terbiye' & a warm mint & Turkish chili flake oil on top.

🍘 Leftover Rice Crackers

Ingredients
 • Leftover cooked rice
 • Soy sauce
 • Oyster sauce
 • Honey
 • Sesame seeds
 • Oil (for frying)

⸻
Steps

1️⃣ Flatten leftover rice into a thin layer.
2️⃣ Cut into circle shapes.
3️⃣ Pan-fry on low heat until crispy.
4️⃣ Mix soy sauce, oyster sauce, and honey.
5️⃣ Brush sauce onto crackers.
6️⃣ Sprinkle with sesame seeds and let it caramelize.

For the first time at Karigar Bazaar – Delhi Edition 2026, we are proud to welcome Ganesh Sahu ji, a master-level Pattachitra artist bringing with him a legacy of devotion, detail, and storytelling through art.

Pattachitra is not just a painting form — it is a centuries-old narrative tradition. From preparing the treated cloth base to creating natural colors by hand, and then rendering intricate, flawless lines that tell mythological and cultural stories — each artwork is a result of days, sometimes weeks, of intense dedication.

Ganesh ji shares that his journey hasn’t been easy. Limited resources, lack of market access, and the rise of machine prints have been constant challenges. Yet, his commitment to keeping this sacred art alive remains unshaken. As he says, “As long as stories live, Pattachitra will live.” ❤️

Meet him in person, witness his live explanations, and understand the patience and precision behind every brushstroke.

Hyderabadi Style Chicken 65 Gravy
Crispy chicken tossed in bold Hyderabadi flavors 🔥

Marination:
500g boneless chicken
1 tbsp ginger-garlic paste
1 tsp red chilli powder
½ tsp turmeric
1 tsp coriander powder
1 tsp garam masala
1 tbsp cornflour
2 tbsp all purpose flour
1 egg + salt
Rest 30 mins
Fry till golden & crispy

Gravy:
Heat 1-2 tsp oil
Sauté garlic, green chillies & curry leaves Add:
2 tbsp yogurt
1 tsp soy sauce
1 tsp red chilli sauce
2 tbsp tomato ketchup
½ tsp vinegar
Cook till gravy thickens
Add fried chicken & toss well
Made with love, served with Hyderabadi spice 🔥

Blackgoat, a popular Singapore hawker stall known for its grilled meat dishes and long queues at Jalan Batu, has announced its permanent closure. In a surprising move, the owners shared more than 10 of their signature recipes online for free, allowing fans to recreate their favorite meals at home and preserve the stall’s culinary legacy.

In a post on Instagram, Blackgoat thanked loyal customers for years of support and hinted that “we’ll be back someday.” The shared recipes that once drew crowds daily.

The recipes provide clear ingredients, preparation steps, and cooking tips, making them accessible for home cooks who want to replicate the stall’s flavors. Many customers praised the owners for openly sharing what are usually closely guarded commercial secrets.

Social media reactions were emotional, with fans expressing sadness over the closure while calling the recipe release generous and heartwarming. Some described it as a meaningful contribution to Singapore’s food culture.

The closure also reflects broader challenges faced by Singapore’s hawker industry, including rising costs and manpower shortages. By sharing their recipes publicly, BlackGoat ensures its flavors live on even after the stall shuts its doors.

🍽️ Recipe | South Indian Easy Potato Roast

• Potatoes – 3 medium size
• Turmeric powder
• Red chilli powder
• Salt

Method:
	1.	Boil 3 medium size potatoes in hot water along with turmeric, chilli powder and salt until half-boiled.
	2.	Drain and keep aside.
	3.	In the same kadai, heat coconut oil.
	4.	Add curry leaves, 4–5 peppercorns, ½ tsp cumin seeds and ½ tsp fennel seeds.
	5.	Add red chilli powder.
	6.	Add the boiled potatoes and sauté on high flame for 2–3 minutes.
	7.	Add salt and pepper as needed.
	8.	Sauté again on high flame for 2–3 minutes.
	9.	Garnish with curry leaves and serve hot 🤍

MRP > ₹20,000 | DIY < ₹3,000 😎
💡 Kids room ke liye full-size wooden pegboard chahiye the (plastic pegboards I didn’t like at all), but online prices dekh ke shock lag gaya 😅 so DIY karne ka decide kiya.😎
🥰 Ek full plyboard liya and usse cut kiya 7 ft × 4 ft which is the size of the pegboard I was looking for. From the left over ply I cut  thin strips for wall frame — isi frame pe baad me pegboard mount hoga.👍
🌟 Phir hammer drill se equidistant holes kiye using 14 mm bit, kyunki same size ke ready-made hardwood dowels online mil gaye the — pegs banana easy ho gaya 🙌 
💫 Frame bhi 1 inch thick aur ply bhi 1 inch thick, isliye peg almost 2 inch andar jaata hai — heavy books, toys ya shelves easily hold kar leta hai. 👍💪
🫶🏼Peg ke ends pe maine wooden domino pieces fix kiye clean finish ke liye (big buttons bhi use kar sakte ho — idea de rahi hoon 😉).
🤟Thoda hole size adjust kiya jahan needed tha aur done ✔️ budget-friendly sturdy wooden diy pegboard ready.
🤜Koi doubt ho toh yahin pooch lo — detailed long video bhi jaldi post hogi.
👉Feedback zaroor dena… aur bolo next kya DIY karun? 🙂

Mindblown. Made this thrice already. 🔥
Peppery, garlicky rasam combined with coconut milk - this shouldn’t work - but it does, and HOW! 

Thengapaal (Coconut Milk) Rasam
Makes 4 Servings ~165 kcal | 2.5g protein | 9g carbs | 13g fat per serving

Ingredients
To crush together 
	•	1 tsp cumin seeds
	•	2 tsp black peppercorns
	•	4–5 garlic cloves
	•	A handful of coriander stems

For the tomato mixture
	•	3 medium whole tomatoes
	•	A small marble-sized ball of tamarind
	•	A handful of fresh coriander leaves
	•	1 sprig curry leaves
	•	A pinch of hing (asafoetida)
	•	¼ tsp turmeric powder
	•	Salt, to taste
	•	1½ cups thin coconut milk
For tempering:
	•	1½ tbsp coconut oil
	•	½ tsp mustard seeds
	•	¼ tsp fenugreek (methi) seeds
	•	2 dry red chillies
	•	1 sprig curry leaves
	•	4–5 shallots, sliced
	•	1 green chilli, slit
To finish:
	•	½–¾ cup thick coconut milk
	•	A pinch of sugar (optional, to balance sourness)
	•	Salt, to adjust

Instructions
	1.	First crush cumin seeds, peppercorns, garlic, and coriander stems using a mortar and pestle. Set aside.
	2.	In another bowl, add whole tomatoes, tamarind, coriander leaves, curry leaves, hing, turmeric, and salt. Crush everything together with your hands till pulpy.
Pour in 1½ cups thin coconut milk, mix well, and set aside.
	3.	Heat coconut oil in a kadhai. Add mustard seeds and let them splutter. Add fenugreek, dry red chillies, and curry leaves.
Toss in the shallots and green chilli, and sauté until shallots turn soft and lightly golden.
	4.	Add the crushed cumin-pepper-garlic mixture and cook for 1–2 minutes until aromatic.
	5.	Reduce the heat and pour in the tomato-coconut mixture. Stir gently and let it come to a gentle simmer (do not boil hard).
It’s okay if it looks slightly split — that’s normal.
	6.	Finish with thick coconut milk:
Turn off the flame. Add ½–¾ cup thick coconut milk and stir slowly to bring everything together.
Adjust seasoning with salt and a tiny pinch of sugar if it tastes too sour.
	7.	Serve hot:
Pour over steaming rice and enjoy with roasted or air-fried pappadams.

.
(Rasam, comfort food, South Indian recipes, coconut milk rasam, healthy Indian recipes)
.

🍃 Usirikaya Pappu ❤️
A comforting Andhra-style dal where soft toor dal meets the bright tang of amla.
🥣 Ingredients (serves 3–4)
Toor dal – ¾ cup (soaked 20–30 mins)
Amla (Indian gooseberry) – 4–5 medium
Onion – 1 small, chopped
Tomato – 1 medium, chopped
Green chillies – 2, slit
Turmeric – ¼ tsp
Salt – to taste
Water – about 2½–3 cups
Tempering
Oil – 1½ tsp
Mustard seeds – ½ tsp
Jeera – ½ tsp
Urad dal – 1 tsp
Hing – a pinch
Curry leaves – 10–12
Garlic – 5–6 cloves, finely chopped
To finish
Fresh coriander leaves – chopped
🍲 Method
📌 In a pressure cooker, add soaked toor dal, onion, tomato, green chillies, whole amla, turmeric, salt, and water.
📌 Pressure cook for 3–4 whistles until the dal turns soft and creamy.
📌 Once the pressure releases, remove the amlas. Deseed them and mash the pulp well. Lightly mash the dal too.
📌 Heat oil in a kadhai. Add mustard seeds and let them splutter. Add jeera, urad dal, hing, curry leaves, and chopped garlic. Sauté until the garlic turns lightly golden and aromatic.
📌 Add the mashed amla to the tempering and sauté briefly.
📌 Add the mashed dal, mix well, and adjust consistency with a little hot water if needed.
📌 Let it simmer gently for 10–15 minutes so the flavours deepen.
📌 Garnish with chopped coriander and serve hot with steamed rice (and a little ghee if you like).
🧮 Approximate Macros
Entire recipe:
Calories: ~540 kcal
Protein: ~31 g
Carbs: ~78 g
Fat: ~19 g
Fiber: ~19 g
Per serving (¼ portion):
Calories: ~135 kcal
Protein: ~7–8 g
Carbs: ~19–20 g
Fat: ~4–5 g
Fiber: ~4–5 g

Fulkopir Roast ❤️ 
Recipe: 
Fry the cauliflower florets in oil for 5-6 minutes. Saute bay leaf, whole garam masala and cumin seeds in oil. Make a paste out of cashew, raisins, white sesame seeds, ginger, green chilies and water. Mix cumin powder, coriander powder, red chilli powder, salt, sugar and yogurt with that paste. Add in the oil and cook until oil separates. Add fried cauliflower and mix well with the masala. Add green peas and hot water. Cook until cauliflower becomes soft. Lastly add ghee and garam masala powder. Enjoy !

Starting the week on a wholesome note with veggies-loaded sabbakki pa-love.

Palav is my all time favourite recipe and this version with the tiny pearls turned out to be a super hit.

Recipe -
- Soak a cup of Nylon sabbakki/sabudana for 3-4 hours or overnight. 
- Transfer to a colander to remove and drain water.
- Make a paste of jeera, saunf, ginger, garlic, green chillies, cardamom, cloves, cinnamon, stone flower, pudina and coriander leaves.
- Heat oil+ghee in a pan, let some mustard and shah jeera splutter.
- Add finely chopped veggies (have used beans, carrot,peas and onion) and fry.
- Add the prepared paste and fry for few minutes. 
- Add strained sabudana/sabbakki and fry until they turn transparent.
- Add salt to taste, lemon juice, mix well and serve .

Dindigul Mushroom Biryani 

🍄‍🟫 Recipe 

Seeraga samba rice - 2 cups
Rinse couple of times and soak for 20 to 30 minutes 

🍄‍🟫 In a heavy bottom pot 

On low heat
Oil - 3 tbsp
Ghee - 3 tbsp
Chili powder - 1 tbsp
Onion - 1 cup (julienne)
Green chilies - 4 to 5 

Turn up the heat and sauté till the onions turn golden 
Bay leaves - 2 
Mint leaves - handful 
Coriander leaves (chopped) - handful
Sauté for a min
Ginger garlic paste - 1 & 1/2 tbsp
Sauté for a min
Shallots (small onion) - 10 (crushed)
Sauté for a min
Yogurt - 1/4 cup
Tomato - 2 small 
Salt to taste
Sauté till the tomatoes turn soft

🍄‍🟫 For biryani masala

Cinnamon - 3 (1 inch sticks)
Cardamom - 10
Cloves - 10 
Starannise - 1 or 2
Cashews - 10
Grind into a powder ( not so fine)

Add it to the pan and cook till the oil separates 
Mushrooms - 300 gms
Cook till the water oozes out and oil separates
Water - 3 cups 
(For 1 cup rice, it’s 1&1/2 cup water) 
Give it a mix
Bring it to boil
Add the soaked rice 
Cook until 3/4th of the water is absorbed 
Add chopped coriander and mint 
Cover and cook for 15 to 20 mins on low heat (simmer)
Turn off the heat
Let it sit for 10 mins before opening the pot 
Enjoy it with onion raita 🤗

Chatpata Pani Puri Makhana 

This one is perfect for tiffins, movie nights, or anytime snack cravings 😊 Makhana is naturally rich in calcium, iron, and antioxidants, making it one of the best snacks to support growing bones and boost immunity

Recipe:
- In an air fryer, dry 1 bowl curry leaves, 1 bunch mint leaves & 1 bowl coriander individually at 180*c for 10-15mins
- They should become nice and crisp
- Now grind them all together with black salt, 4-5 peppercorns, chaat Masala, pani puri masala and some mishri (rock sugar)
- Dry roast 2 bowls of makhana
- Then heat 2 tbsp ghee, add 2 tbsp of the masala and finally the makhana. Mix well till the masala coats the makhana and it’s ready.

Bringing the legendary Nagarjuna style Palak Pappu to your kitchen! 🌿🍲
This isn’t just a recipe; it’s an emotion. This authentic Andhra style spinach dal is the ultimate comfort food—especially when served on a banana leaf with hot rice and ghee. 🥘✨
If you’re looking for a healthy recipe that is truly soul-satisfying, this South Indian dal with its sizzling tadka is a game-changer. Simple, elegant, and packed with traditional Indian flavors.

Save this for your next weekend lunch! 📌

NAGARJUNA style Palak Pappu (Serves 4–5)
Prep Time: 10 minutes
 Cooking Time: 20 minutes
 Total Time: 30 minutes
 Servings: 4–5 people

Ingredients
=============
1 cup toor dal (arhar dal), washed and soaked for 20–30 minutes
4–5 cups chopped palak (spinach)
1 medium onion, roughly chopped
1 medium tomato, roughly chopped
1 tsp ginger-garlic paste
½ tsp turmeric powder
2 tsp coriander powder (dhaniya powder)
½ tsp black pepper powder
Salt to taste
2–2.5 cups water

For Tadka
2 tsp oil
1 tsp mustard seeds (rai)
1 pinch hing (asafoetida)
8–10 curry leaves
2 dry red chillies
½ tsp Kashmiri red chilli powder
2 tsp sambar masala
2 tbsp tamarind pulp (adjust to taste)
2 tbsp fresh coriander, chopped

Method
===========
Add soaked toor dal to a pressure cooker. Add chopped palak, onion, tomato, ginger-garlic paste, turmeric, 2 tsp coriander powder, black pepper, salt and water.
Close the lid and pressure cook for 3–4 whistles until the dal is soft.
Once the pressure releases, lightly mash and mix the dal well.
Heat oil in a pan. Add mustard seeds, hing, curry leaves and dry red chillies.
Add Kashmiri red chilli powder, 2 tsp sambar masala and a little water. Cook for a few seconds.
Pour this tadka into the dal. Add tamarind pulp and mix well.
Garnish with fresh coriander.
.

Designed in the 1970s by Kazuhide Takahama for Sirrah, the Saori Q2 lamp is a striking example of minimalist, architectural lighting. Conceived as both a wall and ceiling light, its large square panel conceals the light source, allowing illumination to emerge subtly through a precise diagonal relief that animates the otherwise pure surface. The result is a calm yet highly conceptual object, where light, shadow, and geometry interact quietly rather than overtly. Influenced by Takahama’s background in Japanese aesthetics and Italian rational design, Saori Q2 transforms light into an abstract spatial element, making it as much a sculptural statement as a functional lamp—an iconic and highly collectible piece of 1970s design.

I’ve been saying for a while that Indian design brands are doing some truly incredible work that’s at par with international levels, especially those blurring the line between furniture and art. @chackoshop is one of those studios creating objects that feel truly one of a kind. This is not an ad or paid for in any way, in fact I had to force the designer to come to my office because I saw this piece and fell in love and needed to see this. 

Mehfil was the piece that stayed with me. A limited-edition, wall-mounted grotto, it feels as much like an artwork as it does a functional object. The doors feature illustrations of three endangered Indian species by @ilcieloinunasogliola, translated into marquetry by @sampigeandco.

The craft behind it is what makes it special. Ten different species of wood are used, with no stains, every colour comes naturally from the timber itself. If you have seen marquetry work being done you can appreciate that this is a very very difficult task. 

Beyond the design, there’s purpose. A portion of every Mehfil sold supports organisations working to protect these endangered species.

Indian design is operating on another level right now. Would you have something like this in your home?

EPISODE 8 | SHIV ROOP – A Hidden Form of Lord Shiva 🕉️

This episode reveals one of the most unknown and hidden forms of Lord Shiva, mentioned in South Indian Shaiva folklore and ancient spiritual storytelling traditions.

In this rare Shiva story, Mahadev takes a maternal form and nourishes 12 crying wild boar babies with his own milk — a powerful symbol of Shiva as the universal protector, nurturer, and Pashupatinath (Lord of all beings).

This is not Vishnu’s Varaha avatar

This is not Shakti’s Varahi form

This is a rare Shaiva legend that shows Lord Shiva beyond destruction, beyond gender, and beyond form — as the source of unconditional compassion and divine motherhood.

Stories like these remind us why Hindu mythology and Shaiva traditions speak of Shiva as both creator and destroyer, father and mother, ascetic and nurturer.

If you are searching for: 

• hidden forms of Shiva

• rare Shiva stories

• South Indian Shiva legends

— this episode is for you.

Dangerous Movie 😈🔥
ഇത് കാണാൻ ധൈര്യം ഉണ്ടോ?

രാത്രി ഒറ്റയ്ക്ക് കാണാൻ ധൈര്യമുണ്ടോ?

EXPLANATION ⏬

In 1791, India trembled with shock. The Sringeri Shankaracharya Math—one of Hinduism’s holiest centers, founded by Adi Shankaracharya himself—was looted and burnt to ashes. 💔🔥The sacred idol of Goddess Sharada was shattered, treasures stolen, and priests fled for their lives. But the attackers were not foreigners… they were Hindu Maratha soldiers under Raghunath Rao Patwardhan. ⚔️

With his monastery in ruins, the Shankaracharya, Sachidananda Bharathi, wrote a desperate plea for help—not to Hindu kings—but to Tipu Sultan, the Muslim ruler of Mysore. 🕌 Tipu’s reply was swift and noble. Outraged by the sacrilege, he sent gold coins, grains, silks, and craftsmen to rebuild the shrine and restore its deity. He personally oversaw the re-consecration and vowed protection for the Math. 🌾💎

For eight years, Tipu maintained warm correspondence with the Shankaracharya, calling him “Jagatguru” and granting full tax exemption to the temple. Historian B.A. Saletore called Tipu "Defender of Hindu Dharma" for this act! 📖✅. Today, the Sri Mookambika Temple at Kollur STILL performs "Salaam Mangalarathi" at 7:30 PM EVERY EVENING in Tipu's honor! 🙏🕯️.. The Srikanteswara Temple in Nanjangud STILL worships the emerald linga (Shiva idol) that Tipu presented! 💎🛕

"Bre Petrunko" is more than just a song; it is a rhythmic explosion of Bulgarian heritage. Performed by the world-renowned ensemble The Great Voices of Bulgaria, this traditional dance song (specifically a Pravoslavno Horo) showcases the breathtaking precision of Balkan polyphonic singing.
Why It Captivates

The track is famous for its uneven meters and the "open-throated" vocal technique that gives Bulgarian folk music its haunting, ethereal edge. The lyrics tell a playful story of a young woman named Petrunka, but the real star is the interlocking harmonies that seem to vibrate through time.

Он не заполняет пространство —
он задаёт ему форму.

Как линии гор, которые держат небо.
Как берег, позволяющий воде быть водой.
Мужчина создаёт контур: устойчивый, спокойный, выверенный.
В нём нет давления — только опора.

А дальше приходит она.
С дыханием, движением, теплом.
Пространство наполняется жизнью — не потому что его украсили,
а потому что в нём стало безопасно быть собой.

Мебель — это тоже про это.
Про форму, которая держит.
Про материю, которая не спорит с энергией, а принимает её.
Про равновесие между силой и мягкостью.

In 2019 China’s diesel demand peaked. It’s been shrinking every single year since.

The property sector implosion made it worse. Real estate was once up to 30% of their economy. Now it’s closer to 18%.

Less construction means fewer trucks and equipment burning diesel. At the same time, sales of LNG-powered trucks started rising.

Demand for diesel evaporated.

..

Now, let’s jump to 2023.

This was the year petrol demand peaked.

Why? The rise of EVs and hybrids. These “new energy vehicles” now account for over half of all car sales in China.

The result was immediate. In 2024, petrol sales cratered by 9% yoy. Chinese crude imports fell for the third time in 5 yrs.

..

That brings us to today’s update.

China’s LNG imports has fallen for 10 straight months.

Authentic Mangalorean CHICKEN SUKKA RECIPE!🍗😋

RECIPE :

Byadagi red chillies - 20
Kashmiri red chillies - 10
Coriander seeds - 3table spoon
Cumin seeds - 1table spoon (Keep some for the coconut mixture later)
Pepper  corn - 6 to 8
Fenugreek - 1/2 teaspoon
Cinnamon - 1 inch
Cloves - 6 to 7
Cardamom  2
Star anise - 1
Dry roast the above spices
Then add Onion small - 1
Garlic - 12 cloves
Tamarind - 1 marble
Turmeric - 1tsp
Grind the above spices nicely.

In a vessel, add 2 table spoon coconut oil.
1 table spoon ghee
1 tsp mustered seeds 
10 Cloves garlic
1 onion
15 curry leaves
Coriander leaves
Chicken - 2kg
Salt as per taste

once chicken is 50% cooked, add the Masala to it

For the coconut mixture:
1 big grated coconut
2 big onions
1/2 table spoon roasted Cumin seeds
Grind this coarsely and not into a paste

Then add this coconut mixture into the chicken and bring it to a boil.
Your Authentic Mangalorean Chicken Sukka is ready😋

𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗩𝗮𝗿𝘂𝗻𝗮-𝗔𝗵𝘂𝗿𝗮 𝗠𝗮𝘇𝗱𝗮 𝗰𝗼𝗻𝗻𝗲𝗰𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 - 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗜𝗻𝗱𝗼 𝗜𝗿𝗮𝗻𝗶𝗮𝗻 𝗦𝗰𝗵𝗶𝘀𝗺 (Varuna-Mitra Part II): You may have heard that 𝗔𝗵𝘂𝗿𝗮 𝗠𝗮𝘇𝗱𝗮, 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗭𝗼𝗿𝗼𝗮𝘀𝘁𝗿𝗶𝗮𝗻 𝘀𝘂𝗽𝗿𝗲𝗺𝗲 𝗱𝗲𝗶𝘁𝘆 𝗵𝗮𝘀 𝟭𝟬𝟭 𝗻𝗮𝗺𝗲𝘀 - 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗼𝗻𝗲 𝗼𝗳 𝘁𝗵𝗲𝘀𝗲 𝗶𝘀 '𝗩𝗮𝗿𝘂𝗻'. Yes - indeed cognate with Vedic Varuna. Both of them also have a 'twin' solar deity attached to them known as Mitra (Vedic)/Mithra (Zoroastrian).

✹ 𝙏𝙬𝙤 𝙂𝙤𝙙𝙨 - 𝙗𝙤𝙩𝙝 𝙨𝙤𝙫𝙚𝙧𝙚𝙞𝙜𝙣 𝙞𝙣 𝙑𝙚𝙙𝙞𝙘 𝙄𝙣𝙙𝙞𝙖 𝙖𝙣𝙙 𝘼𝙫𝙚𝙨𝙩𝙖𝙣 𝙄𝙧𝙖𝙣. Shared ritual grammar bound these two cultures - later taught to see each other as enemies, due to the religious 𝗶𝗻𝗳𝗶𝗴𝗵𝘁𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗯𝗲𝘁𝘄𝗲𝗲𝗻 𝘁𝘄𝗼 𝗮𝗻𝗰𝗶𝗲𝗻𝘁 𝗥𝗶𝘀𝗵𝗶-𝘀𝗮𝗴𝗲𝘀! How? This video traces the Indo-Iranian schism through Varuṇa, Mithra, Indra, and the peoples remembered as Dasyus and Anus - drawing directly from the Rigveda, Atharvaveda, and Zend Avesta. I give you an introductory overview into this 𝗮𝗻𝗰𝗶𝗲𝗻𝘁 𝗹𝗶𝘁𝘂𝗿𝗴𝗶𝗰𝗮𝗹 𝗱𝗶𝘀𝗮𝗴𝗿𝗲𝗲𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁 𝗶𝗻 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗜𝗻𝗱𝗼-𝗜𝗿𝗮𝗻𝗶𝗮𝗻 𝘀𝗽𝗵𝗲𝗿𝗲, as the second of five parts - outlining this conflict, coming soon!

✹ The focal artwork here is 𝗟𝗼𝗿𝗱𝘀 𝗩𝗮𝗿𝘂𝗻𝗮-𝗠𝗶𝘁𝗿𝗮: 𝗦𝗼𝘃𝗲𝗿𝗲𝗶𝗴𝗻 𝗡𝗼𝗯𝗹𝗲 𝗔𝘀𝘂𝗿𝗮𝘀 𝗼𝗳 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗗𝗲𝘃𝗮𝘀 (2023) by @achintya.venkatesh as part of my ongoing 𝙑𝙚𝙙𝙞𝙘 𝙋𝙖𝙣𝙩𝙝𝙚𝙤𝙣 𝙑𝙞𝙨𝙞𝙤𝙣𝙨 series: join me as I illustrate obscure Vedic Hindu lore!

My take on Zero-Oil, High-Protein Make & Freeze Shami Kebab

crispy outside, juicy inside and packed with protein.
Each kebab has about 14–15g of protein, so you can enjoy them without stressing about oil or calories.
Healthy food doesn’t have to be boring.

Ingredients:
	•	Mutton – 500 g
	•	Soaked chana dal – 300 g
	•	Ginger garlic paste – 1 tbsp
	•	Onion – 1 big (chopped)
	•	Green chilli – 1 (chopped)
	•	Red chilli powder – 1 tbsp
	•	Salt – to taste
	•	Coriander powder – 1 tsp
	•	Cumin powder – 1 tsp
	•	Turmeric powder – ½ tsp
	•	Chilli flakes – 1 tsp
	•	Garam masala – ½ tsp
	•	Kabab masala – optional
	•	Water – 2 cups
	•	Mint leaves – handful (chopped)
	•	Coriander leaves – handful (chopped)
	•	Chaat masala – 1 tsp
	•	Egg – 1
	•	Boiled egg – for stuffing (optional)
	•	Ghee – for greasing/brushing

⸻

Method:
	1.	Pressure cook:
Add mutton, soaked chana dal, onion, ginger garlic paste, all spices, salt and water into a pressure cooker.
Cook on low heat for 30 minutes or until everything becomes soft.
	2.	Mash:
Let it cool slightly, then mash or blend to a thick mixture (no water left).
	3.	Mix:
Add mint, coriander leaves, chaat masala and 1 raw egg.
Mix well.
	4.	Shape kababs:
Take some mixture in your hand.
Place a slice of boiled egg in the center and shape into a kabab.
	5.	Air fry:
Grease air fryer basket with ghee.
Place kababs and brush ghee on top.
	6.	Cook:
Air fry at 190°C for 10 minutes per side (total 20 minutes) until golden and crispy.

⸻

✨ Serve hot with mint chutney, onions, and lemon!

Ayahuasca is not just Banisteriopsis caapi.

From the Ayahuasca vine come three key alkaloids:
• Harmine (Telepathine)
• Harmaline
• Tetrahydroharmine (THH)
These alkaloids prepare the body and mind, allowing the medicine to work gently and deeply.
From the Chakruna leaf (Psychotria viridis) comes DMT. 
Because these elements work together so powerfully, the first night is always approached with care.
A small dose.
A moment of listening.
To see how the body and energy respond.
This work is done with respect, patience, and responsibility as it has been taught for generations in the Amazon.

Peak anime doesn’t trend ✨
It doesn’t dominate timelines.
And it definitely doesn’t beg for attention 👀

The greatest anime experiences are often hidden in plain sight—buried under algorithms, overshadowed by hype, and missed by most viewers chasing what’s popular.
Titles like Gankutsuou, Shiki, Hyouge Mono, Texhnolyze, Deca-Dence, and other underrated masterpieces prove that true peak anime prioritizes writing, atmosphere, themes, and artistic risk over mass appeal.

These are anime that demand patience.
Anime that trust the viewer.
Anime that don’t explain everything—but mean everything.

If you’re tired of surface-level stories and want anime with depth, symbolism, bold direction, and lasting impact, this is where peak really lives.
Not in popularity—but in intention.

If you’re ready to dig deeper into the hidden side of anime greatness, follow @animecultivated.

Photographs of Ganja & Op*um shops from the subcontinent, 1960s-70s.  There was a time when government-licensed cannabis (Ganja) and op*um shops legally operated across India, Nepal, and Bangladesh.

In India, cannabis has ancient roots — and state-run bhang shops still exist today.

Nepal once ran legal hashish stores in Kathmandu until 1973, before global pressure shut them down.
Bangladesh, too, had licensed ganja outlets during the colonial and Pakistan eras, before strict bans followed.

For centuries, cannabis and op*um were not underground substances in the Indian subcontinent — they were regulated, taxed, and openly sold under state supervision. During the colonial era, licensed ganja, bhang, and opium shops operated across India, Nepal, and present-day Bangladesh, generating revenue and remaining woven into cultural and medicinal life.

The shift to blanket bans reshaped the subcontinent. Legal revenue disappeared, traditional farmers were criminalized, and black markets expanded in their place. Cultural practices were pushed underground, enforcement costs rose, and cross-border trafficking increased — creating challenges that still persist today.

Day 60 of 100 Must Watch Anime Movies ✨

A strangely gentle mystery, two awkward girls, a missing classmate. It almost feels unscripted.

🎥 Anime Movie: The Case of Hana and Alice
🍿 Studio: Studio Colorido (A Whisker Away)
📝 Director: Shunji Iwai (Love Letter)

The Flowers of Evil (Aku no Hana) is easily one of the most polarizing and unsettling anime ever made.
It’s a psychological horror-drama that feels more like a fever dream or an experimental indie film than a traditional anime.The story follows Takao Kasuga, a middle school bookworm who is obsessed with the French poetry collection Les Fleurs du mal by Charles Baudelaire. He views himself as “different” and more intellectual than his peers.  
One day, in a moment of madness, he steals the gym clothes of his crush, Nanako Saeki. He is caught in the act by Sawa Nakamura, the class outcast and “creep.” Instead of reporting him, she blackmails him into a “contract,” forcing him to embrace his darkest, most “perverted” impulses as she systematically tears his life apart. #100days #anime #otaku #animescenes #edit

Peptides are reshaping hair-loss treatment by moving beyond one-size-fits-all solutions and targeting the biological signals that actually regulate hair growth. 

Unlike traditional drugs that broadly alter hormones or blood flow, peptides work as precise messengers that can activate dormant follicles, extend the anagen (growth) phase of the hair cycle, and reduce inflammation at the scalp level. 

This specificity allows treatments to be tailored to different causes of hair loss—such as stress, aging, or androgen sensitivity—while minimizing systemic side effects.

DHARMASTHALA TEMPLE STYLE THILI SAARU🙏🌸

Temple food has a way of feeling different.
Simple ingredients, cooked with intention. This Thili Saaru from Dharmasthala is light, comforting and deeply satisfying. Best enjoyed with hot rice and ghee.

RECIPE:
Made with 💗 by Annapoorna

Masala to roast:
2tsp coriander seeds
1.5 tsp jeera
1.5 tsp pepper
1/4 tsp methi
3-4 byadgi chilli

Grind the masala until fine. Add half cup grated coconut and grind again. In the meanwhile take a handful of toor dal cook and set aside. 

Add the roasted masala to a kadhai. Add water to bring it into a rasam consistency. Now add the cooked toor dal. Mix well. Next add tamarind juice( made out 1 small ball of tamarind). Add 3-4tsp of jaggery powder and mix well. Add salt to taste. Bring this to a boil.

For tadka, use simple tempering of mustard seeds, curry leaves and red chilli. Serve with hot rice and enjoy the divine taste.

Terpenes explained: Myrcene 🌱

🌱 What it is
Myrcene is one of the most abundant terpenes found in cannabis. It’s known for its earthy, musky aroma, often described as clove-like or ripe, and becomes especially pungent at higher concentrations.

🌿 Beyond cannabis
You’ll also find myrcene in plants like mango, thyme, lemongrass, and hops — linking familiar scents across species.

🧠 What science shows
Across published studies, β-myrcene is frequently examined for biological activity related to relaxation, pain response, and inflammation. Much of this work is preclinical, but several independent research groups have reported consistent findings across models.

⚙️ Why myrcene matters
Myrcene doesn’t raise potency — it helps shape the experience. Its bioactive, lipophilic nature allows it to interact easily with biological systems, and profiles rich in myrcene often correlate with heavier, body-focused effects in cannabis. These are correlations, not proven causal effects.

🔍 The takeaway
Understanding terpenes like myrcene moves us beyond THC percentages and closer to how cannabis actually works in real life.

Re-representation of "Cat stealing Prawn" 
Drawing inspiration from the traditional Kalighat Patachitra of Bengal and the iconic modernist style of Jamini Roy, this piece reimagines a classic folk motif with bold lines and cultural depth. 

Rooted in the whimsical yet biting satire of Kalighat Patachitra, this figure represents a cat posing as a saint. It is a nod to Bengali folklore, unmasking those who use the guise of spirituality to hide their earthly appetites. The image has been made popular through the paintings of Jamini Roy—one of the earliest modern artists from Bengal—but the iconography dates back to the days of Kalighat patachitra paintings, a unique traditional genre of paintings that was developed in Bengal, in the nineteenth century, although its origins go back much further in time - DAG

Southekai Tambuli 🥒🥥
(Cucumber Tambuli from Coastal Karnataka)

This is not a raita and not a curry — tambuli lives in that beautiful in-between. Cooling, lightly spiced, coconut-forward, and quietly comforting. Fresh cucumber, raw-ground coconut, yogurt, and a fragrant mustard–curry leaf tempering — soothing without being boring.

How it’s made:

Grind ½ cup fresh grated coconut, ½ cup peeled and chopped tender cucumber (about 80 g), 1 small green chilli, ¼ tsp black peppercorns, and ½ tsp cumin seeds with 2–3 tbsp water into a smooth paste.

In a bowl, whisk ½ cup fresh thick curd (120 g) with salt to taste. Add the ground paste and thin it with ½ cup water (100–120 ml) till you get a pourable, lightly flowing consistency — like thin raita or drinkable yogurt.

For the tempering, heat 2 tsp coconut oil. Add ½ tsp mustard seeds and let them splutter. Add 1 small dried red chilli (broken) and 8–10 curry leaves. Pour this immediately over the tambuli.

Mix gently and serve right away — slightly chilled or at room temperature. Best with hot rice, a simple vegetable side, or just on its own when you want something cooling and nourishing.

🎥: @_ctankep_

While pithla and zunka bhakri are well known across Maharashtra, Kulith Pithla made with horse gram flour remains a lesser-known Konkan favourite.

Recipe:
In a bowl, add kulith (horse gram) flour and about 4 times water, mixing in batches to avoid lumps.
Heat oil in a pan. Add mustard seeds, curry leaves, hing, and chopped onions. Sauté well.
Add the kulith paste and loosen with remaining water.
Season with salt and kokam for tang.
Cover and cook until thick, glossy, and perfectly creamy.

Serve hot with bhaakri—and yes, I am judging if you’re not adding fluffy, melt-in-the-mouth loni on the side.

Amma’s Samayal: Ep 5 - Paruppu Usuli ✨
(Serves 5-6 people)

Ingredients:
Daal Paste
	•	1 cup moong dal (soaked for 30 mins)
	•	½ cup channa dal (soaked for 30 mins)
	•	1 heaped tbsp chilli powder
	•	1 tsp turmeric powder
	•	A pinch of hing
	•	1 tbsp rock salt
	•	2 tbsp gingelly oil (Sesame oil)

Vegetable
	•	1 kg beans, finely chopped

Tadka
	•	3–4 tbsp gingelly oil (Sesame oil)
	•	1.5 tsp mustard seeds
	•	2 tsp urad dal
	•	3–4 dried red chillies
	•	8–10 curry leaves
	•	½ tsp hing

Method:

1. Make the dal paste
Grind everything under Daal Paste into a smooth, fine mixture. Grease a steam pan generously with gingelly oil (Sesame oil), pour in the paste, and drizzle another 2 tbsp of gingelly oil (Sesame oil) over the top. Swirl gently so the oil spreads evenly.

2. Steam the dal
Pressure cook for 5 whistles or steam for 30 minutes until the dal is firm and fully cooked. Scoop it onto a plate and let it cool completely.
Once cool, grind into a fine lentil crumble.

3. As the daal cools, steam/cook the beans with a little salt
Steam the chopped beans with a little salt until just tender, then set aside.

4. Temper and combine
Heat gingelly oil (Sesame oil) in a pan. Add mustard seeds and let them splutter. Add urad dal, dried red chillies, curry leaves, and hing.
Once aromatic, add the lentil crumble and sauté for a minute. Add the cooked beans, mix well, and adjust salt if needed.

Serve hot - the perfect accompaniment for Vatha Kozhambu.
